item.title: Hamas says it will step aside. Here's what it means for Gaza — and what obstacles remain
item.description: While the move could reshape who governs Gaza, experts say it does not resolve the core dispute preventing a lasting peace.

item.title: 'Shaped the lives of thousands': The teacher who never stopped showing up
item.description: The longest-serving Aboriginal Education Officer in NSW, Aunty Ruth Simms, has inspired generations in and out of the classroom.

item.title: Inside Narendra Modi's Australia visit: Optics are warm. Politics are harder
item.description: The applause, the ambition and the tensions shaping Narendra Modi's Australia visit.

item.title: Some Triple Zero calls impacted, minister confirms, as Telstra says software glitch caused major outage
item.description: Telstra has been forced to carry out hundreds of welfare checks after an hours-long outage that disrupted everything from phone services to trains.

item.title: Telstra says services are coming back online | SBS News in Easy English 8 July 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for English language learners

item.title: The European country preparing for a Russian attack, as Putin weighs odds
item.description: Poland says the coming months are "critical", but experts say Russia is cruising for a bruising if it provokes war on two fronts.

item.title: How could a mobile outage disrupt trains? An expert explains
item.description: Here are the underlying tech connections between a telecommunications network crash and rail service disruptions.

item.title: Telstra shares hit February low following network outage
item.description: The Australian sharemarket opened sharply lower as renewed hostilities in the Middle East rattled investors, before recovering most of its losses by the close. Energy stocks surged on a jump in oil prices, while Telstra slid after a network outage sent its shares to their lowest level since February. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Francesco De Stradis from Ord Minnett about the day's market moves and what investors should watch next.

item.title: Trump says ceasefire agreement is 'over' as US launches renewed strikes on Iran
item.description: The United States has initiated a new wave of attacks in the Middle East for the second consecutive day.

item.title: INTERVIEW: What does Australia's latest health report reveal ?
item.description: The Australian Institute of Health and Welfare has released its 2026 health report, revealing the key drivers of health outcomes across the country, and where targeted attention is most needed. Researchers say Australians born today are expected to live longer than previous generations, cancer survival rates are improving, and coronary heart disease deaths are decreasing. But head of the Institute's Flagship Reporting Unit Melanie Dunford says the findings also show Australia is facing some significant health challenges and persistent inequalities

item.title: 'Dead pigs': The coded language inside a 'ruthless' network of online sexual predators in Germany
item.description: A Berlin court has convicted a Chinese man over his alleged involvement in an online network of sexual predators bragging and planning attacks on Telegram. The Telegram networks uncovered by German investigators are understood to have thousands of participants in a case echoing that of Gisele Pelicot in France.

item.title: India's Prime Minister visits Australia | SBS News in Easy English 8 July 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for English language learners

item.title: ABC, SBS questioned on Gaza coverage by Royal Commission on Antisemitism
item.description: The public broadcasters answered questions about their coverage of the Middle East, antisemitism and social cohesion.

item.title: Trump may have offered Ukraine exactly what it wants. But there's a major catch
item.description: Donald Trump has offered to let Ukraine produce high-powered missiles — but what is the US president trying to achieve from the deal?

item.title: Trump says the Iran deal is over, an angry NATO lovefest and has Hamas given up politics?
item.description: Donald Trump calls the Iranian regime ‘scum’ as the US and Iran trade strikes. What's next for Iran's leadership as the country gives Ayatollah Ali Khamenei a long goodbye. Hamas agrees to dissolves its government, but will that change things? Also, Trump gives his NATO allies a kicking and a hug as Ukraine gets the nod to produce Patriot missiles. Plus, mystery balls plague another Australian beach.

item.title: War jitters hit the market as Australia’s biggest IPO of the year makes a strong debut
item.description: The Australian sharemarket fell for a fourth straight session as escalating conflict in the Middle East pushed oil prices higher and weighed on investor sentiment, while energy stocks outperformed and Telstra gained ground. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Nick Schoenmaker from Portfolio Construction Management about the day’s market moves, and Nick Schoenmaker also explains what the strong debut of construction group FDC Consolidated, Australia’s biggest IPO of the year, could mean for the future pipeline of sharemarket listings.

item.title: Researchers discover new clue that helps explain severe COVID-19
item.description: New research has uncovered a previously unknown route the virus can use inside the body.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Controversial UK politician Nigel Farage forces by-election over financial allegations
item.description: One of Britain's most controversial politicians and leader of the right-wing Reform Party, Nigel Farage, has announced he is quitting his seat so he can clear his name over financial allegations. His resignation has triggered a by-election in the Essex seat of Clacton - but none of the major parties plan to stand against him. At this stage, there is only one satirical opponent standing - Count Binface, who always appears in public with a dustbin on his head. Mr Farage was a key figure in the campaign for Britain to leave the EU. A donation of five million pounds by a supporter based in Thailand has seen accusations that Mr Farage should have declared it to Parliament, but didn't. He claims the establishment and the media are using “foul means” to stop him. Tim Bale is professor of politics at Queen Mary University of London and he told the Associated Press Mr Farage is taking quite a gamble.

item.title: 'Lot of questions' for Telstra; death found to be unrelated to Triple Zero outage
item.description: Telstra has rejected claims that Wednesday's mass outage was related to its job cuts.

item.title: Police investigating death after Telstra outage | SBS News in Easy English 10 July 2026
item.description: A bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Andy Burham set to become British PM: but can Count Binface dispose of Nigel Farage?
item.description: Andy Burnham has taken another leap toward replacing Keir Starmer as UK Prime Minister, after securing 322 nominations from his party. Reform UK's Nigel Farage has also dramatically announced he will resign as a member of parliament, forcing a snap by-election. The populist leader's only confirmed rival in the seat of Clacton is a comedian who goes by the name 'Count Binface'.

item.title: If the US-Iran war reignites, how badly could Australia be impacted?
item.description: What could it mean for Australia if the US-Iran ceasefire continues to unravel and fighting intensifies once again?

item.title: Westpac brings forward rate cut forecast as ASX snaps losing streak.
item.description: Ricardo Gonçalves examines why Westpac Chief Economist Luci Ellis now expects the RBA to begin cutting interest rates in 2027 instead of 2028, while still forecasting two more rate rises before then. Plus, the Australian sharemarket ends a four-day losing streak, with miners leading the gains as gold and uranium stocks rally, while Telstra slips further following its recent network outage. Dianne Colledge from Morgans also joins the podcast to break down the day's market moves and discusses the latest wave of US IPOs.

item.title: Rate cuts could come earlier than expected — but there's a catch
item.description: One of Australia's leading banks says rate cuts could come sooner than expected. Plus: petrol prices, a big ASX IPO and a Telstra outage.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Tuna Wars: Allan Rahari talks about the fishery's major threats
item.description: Allan Rahari, the Interim Deputy Director General of the Pacific Islands Forum Fisheries Agency and the Director of Fisheries Operations. He calls tuna the "backbone" of the Pacific and speaks with SBS News about a major threat to the fishery: illegal, unreported and unregulated fishing.

item.title: The 'invisible migrants' no-one talked about — until now
item.description: The untold stories of Dutch migrants who made Australia home after World War Two.

item.title: Hobart's MONA museum plans Bangkok expansion
item.description: The Premier of Tasmania said he hoped the project would strengthen Tasmania's ties with Thailand.

item.title: Nearly two million evacuated as Typhoon Bavi barrels towards China
item.description: Two landslides triggered by heavy rains driven have killed 17 people in the Philippines.

item.title: Anthony's city apartment costs the same as the Brisbane sharehouse he rented 20 years ago
item.description: Vienna has spent decades investing in a social housing system that supports half its residents. Could Australia replicate it?

item.title: 'An all-out assault': How Donald Trump is trying to reshape American elections
item.description: Amid Donald Trump's sweeping efforts to change voting rules, the midterms could have lasting consequences for US elections.

item.title: Central Australia's buffel grass problem
item.description: Record rainfall in Australia's outback this year has transformed a usually red desert landscape into a green oasis. The sight can even be seen from space. And while it's welcomed by some - environmentalists and Traditional Owners say, in this instance, green is not good.

item.title: Tuna Wars: one of the world's most dangerous jobs
item.description: This is the second part of our special series about tuna fishing in the Pacific. According to fisheries officials in the Pacific, one of the most difficult to pin down breaches when it comes to overfishing is the ability to monitor catch - and ensure compliance with fishing licences. In an effort to combat this - fisheries observers are stationed on many commercial fishing vessels to keep watch - but it is also considered one of the most dangerous jobs in the world. And this story was supported by the Pulitzer Centre.

item.title: South Africa World Cup midfielder Jayden Adams dies at the age of 25
item.description: Jayden Adams played in three matches at the FIFA World Cup.

item.title: US congressman says he was detained by Israeli settlers in occupied West Bank
item.description: Ro Khanna says he was confronted and detained by Israeli settlers brandishing US-made assault rifles.

item.title: What we know about the 2030 men's FIFA World Cup
item.description: The 2026 FIFA World Cup has reached the pointy end, but planning for the next tournament is already well underway.

item.title: I gave up finding my biological father. Then, a Christmas gift changed everything
item.description: Debbie spent over five decades trying to find out who her biological father was. Two years ago, she got her answer.

item.title: New standards force Australian universities to address racism in sector overhaul
item.description: The changes were part of a landmark report that found discrimination against 90 per cent of Jewish and Palestinian students.

item.title: How Australia's digital arrival cards will work — and why travellers may still need paper
item.description: Australia is modernising the arrival card system — but the familiar orange form is not totally disappearing.

item.title: National rollout of digital passenger cards for international travellers | SBS News in Easy English 13 July 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for English language learners.

item.title: National rollout of digital passenger cards for international travellers | SBS News in Easy English 13 July 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for English language learners.

item.title: Auction clearance rates have climbed — but there's a 'brutal reality' behind the bounce
item.description: At first glance, the latest auction figures look encouraging. But experts say they only tell part of the story.

item.title: Unexpected passing of Trump ally Lindsey Graham sends shocks through Republican party ahead of midterms
item.description: Influential US Senator Lindsey Graham has died suddenly at the age of 71, sending shockwaves through the Republican party and casting doubt on the midterms in South Carolina. Once a staunch opponent turned loyal ally to Donald Trump, Mr Graham is being remembered for his quick wit, influence on Trump, and hawkish US foreign policy.

item.title: South Australia unveils plan to outlaw violent offenders from dating apps
item.description: A warning - the following story contains elements that may distress some people. South Australia has unveiled a nation-first plan to ban domestic violence and sex offenders from dating apps. It follows a report released by the Australian Institute of Criminology in 2022 that found a third of respondents were subjected to sexual violence by someone they had met on an app or website in the previous five years.

item.title: 'Immeasurable loss': Actor Sam Neill dies at 78
item.description: Celebrities have shared tributes to the New Zealand actor, whose family said the loss was "sudden and unexpected".

item.title: 'You've got no choice': The $1,475 fee leaving some in Australia feeling 'captive'
item.description: A steep visa hike has turned the right to travel — and return home — into a costly obligation.

item.title: What's in a name? New racism standards come into effect as unis grapple with antisemitism definition
item.description: Universities will be required to develop definitions of racism, including antisemitism, Islamophobia and prejudice against Indigenous people, under changes that come into effect next year. It coincides with hearings of the Royal Commission into Antisemitism and Social Cohesion where Jewish students have detailed their experiences on campus.

item.title: Oil surges as another AI company makes solid sharemarket debut
item.description: A flat finish for the Australian sharemarket masked a volatile trading session, with the ASX spending most of the day in negative territory as Brent crude oil jumped 5 per cent on renewed uncertainty in the Middle East. The session also followed the blockbuster US debut of another AI-related company, raising fresh questions about where the next investment opportunities in artificial intelligence could emerge. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Ben Lam, Head of Equities at Colonial First State, about the outlook for AI investing and the day’s market moves.

item.title: How Australia's first dating app ban for sex offenders would work
item.description: Millions of Australians use online dating apps. An advocate says "seeking connection shouldn't come at the cost of your safety".

item.title: As Australia closes in on eliminating one cancer, another is showing up earlier in life
item.description: The latest national data reveals a shifting cancer landscape — and a generation caught on both sides of it.

item.title: US strikes Iran for third night as Trump says deal still 'possible' while announcing Hormuz fees
item.description: Iran's foreign minister has declared that his country will be the Strait of Hormuz's guardian "forever".

item.title: UN accuses Hamas of hampering aid as EU pledges billions to rebuild Gaza
item.description: The UN says relief operations had become increasingly dangerous amidst an on-going humanitarian crisis.

item.title: Tributes flow for 'gentleman' honorary Aussie, Sam Neill
item.description: Tributes have poured in to remember revered actor Sam Neill, who died on Monday at the age of 78. The New Zealand star had become beloved for his roles in a range of Hollywood and Australian films, including Jurassic Park, My Brilliant Career, and The Dish.

item.title: Australian unis to face fines for failure to act on racism | SBS News in Easy English 14 July 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for English language learners.

item.title: Tensions rise in the Strait of Hormuz as Trump reinstates Iranian port blockade
item.description: The United States has launched fresh strikes on Iran, as the battle over one of the world's busiest shipping lanes intensifies. Donald Trump has reinstated a blockade on Iranian shipping, after two oil tankers were hit in the Strait of Hormuz. Iran is warning it will not allow the United States to take control of the strategic waterway.

item.title: 'World's most prestigious retirement home': Does the US Congress have an age problem?
item.description: The current US Congress is the third oldest in history, with senior Senators increasingly holding on to the job for longer. With the recent death of Lindsey Graham and hospitalisation of Mitch McConnell, the debate has revived around an age limit for Congress, which the majority of Americans support.

item.title: Trump claimed Iran's nuclear sites were 'obliterated'. Now another is in his sights
item.description: Buried under 600m of granite, the site is assessed as beyond the reach of even the US' most powerful bunker-buster bombs.

item.title: Twin threats to petrol prices are looming, but that's not the whole story
item.description: Donald Trump's Strait of Hormuz threat and the end of the government's fuel excise could see petrol prices rise, but by how much?

item.title: Oil surges most since 2020, stokes inflation fears
item.description: The S&P/ASX 200 has closed flat, despite Donald Trump saying the US would reinstate a blockade on Iranian ships transiting through the Strait of Hormuz and impose a 20 per cent toll fee. As a result, crude prices surged in their biggest one-day increase since 2020. For more, Stephanie Youssef spoke with Marcus Today senior market analyst Henry Jennings. The latest consumer sentiment reading by Westpac and the Melbourne Institute has shown confidence rose to 83.9 in July, as lower petrol prices and easing interest rate expectations provided some relief for households, though pessimists still outnumber optimists. For more, Stephanie Youssef spoke with Westpac senior economist Matthew Hassan.

item.title: When the man Vikki thought was her dad opened the door, she knew something wasn’t right
item.description: With more people taking DNA tests to search for family, there are concerns about data breaches, privacy and risks to relationships.

item.title: 'Should not have to beg': The target 20 major super funds failed to meet
item.description: A new mystery shopper study of 1,000 calls found major super funds routinely fall short on empathy and basic support.

item.title: Australia is changing course on AI — but key questions remain
item.description: Labor wants to accelerate AI investment while protecting households, workers and creators — but crucial details are still to come.

item.title: Australia is changing course on AI — but key questions remain
item.description: Labor wants to accelerate AI investment while protecting households, workers and creators — but crucial details are still to come.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Has Australia's definition of antisemitism been 'weaponised'?
item.description: The International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance's working definition of antisemitism has become one of the world's most influential and contested tools for identifying anti-Jewish hatred. Adopted by universities, public institutions, and governments across the globe, including here in Australia, supporters argue it helps recognise antisemitism in a consistent way, while critics say it can be used to blur the line between antisemitism and legitimate criticism of the state of Israel. Kenneth Stern is one of the lead drafters of the IHRA definition and a former director on antisemitism and extremism for the American Jewish Committee. He says the definition has been "weaponised" in ways he never intended, particularly when it's used to regulate speech or shut down debate on university campuses, the media and elsewhere. He's speaking to SBS reporter Tee Mitchell

item.title: New plan for AI regulation in Australia | SBS News in Easy English 15 July 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for English language learners.

item.title: New Iran, US attacks amid battle over control of Hormuz
item.description: Donald Trump has abandoned plans to charge ships using the Strait of Hormuz, just a day after announcing the controversial proposal. His administration has pressed ahead with a renewed blockade of Iranian shipping and another round of military strikes, but the U.S. President says that instead of the fee scheme, Gulf states will instead invest in the United States. Iran insists the ceasefire agreement reached last month has effectively collapsed, as both sides continue to exchange attacks.

item.title: All the records broken during the 2026 FIFA World Cup
item.description: From historic debuts to ground-breaking careers, record after record has fallen this World Cup.

item.title: "A generational opportunity": PM unveils new national standards for artificial intelligence in major speech
item.description: In a major speech, the Prime Minister has outlined plans for a set of new national standards for artificial intelligence. The federal government will establish an Office of Artificial Intelligence as part of the new national framework. Anthony Albanese says Australia can encourage investment while managing risk to capitalise on the technology.

item.title: What happens next in the Strait of Hormuz could set a dangerous precedent
item.description: The fight over a vital shipping route could reshape how global chokepoints are used in future conflicts, experts say.

item.title: Deadly avian flu detected in New Zealand for the first time
item.description: The alert followed a migratory seabird near Wellington returning a postive test result.

item.title: US inflation eases as China's economy slows
item.description: A rise in BHP shares and a record for Macquarie helped to push the Australian sharemarket 0.4 per cent higher as inflation in the US eased while China's economy slowed. SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with James Gerrish from Market Partners to find out what this means for investors and the outlook for interest rates.

item.title: Australian patient details exposed in GP network cyberattack
item.description: Partnered Health said that 21 clinics across several Australian cities had been affected by a cybersecurity breach.

item.title: One mistaken sighting created an enduring myth about this Aussie animal
item.description: It has been credited with soaring 100 metres. But new research suggests the greater glider's reputation was overblown.

item.title: 96 people died in the Hillsborough disaster in 1989. Now a new UK law has been named in their memory
item.description: Britain has passed landmark legislation aimed at preventing state cover-ups and improving accountability after public disasters. Prime Minister Keir Starmer, who promised to bring in the law during his 2024 election campaign, opened the debate on the bill in the Commons in one of his final acts as leader. The bill was inspired and named after the Hillsborough disaster in 1989 when 96 Liverpool fans were killed in a crowd crush.

item.title: Argentina faces FIFA fine after players celebrate win against England with Falklands banner
item.description: The banner referred to the disputed territory at the centre of the 1982 war between Argentina and the United Kingdom.

item.title: Lettuce, Taco Bell, and 7,000 cases: An 'explosive' problem is ripping through the US
item.description: Health authorities in dozens of states are scrambling to trace the outbreak's origins.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 16 July 2026
item.description: A daily 5 minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Government backs its AI pitch as debate continues over 'energy vampire' data centres
item.description: There have been mixed reactions to the federal government's plan for a national regulatory framework for the Artificial Intelligence industry, to come into effect in the new year. Environmental advocates have issued warnings over data centres' high use of water and energy, while unions have cautiously welcomed the plan, hoping it will create more local jobs.

item.title: In pictures: Orange skies and wildfire smoke loom over the World Cup final
item.description: As thick smoke settles in over major cities, Canada recorded the worst air quality in the world.

item.title: US and Iran in dire Strait as they fight for control, Ukraine’s missile muscle & South Korea's matchmaking monks
item.description: The ceasefire crumbles as the US tries to loosen Iran’s grip on the Strait of Hormuz with bombing. Ukraine and its European allies join forces to protect the continent from ballistic missiles. Plus, how will the death of US Senator Lindsey Graham impact US foreign policy? And would you let South Korea’s Buddhist monks lead you to love?

item.title: ASX flat as BHP strike and production outlook weighs on market
item.description: A stronger year for ASX listings is offering signs of renewed confidence, but competition for IPOs remains fierce as companies increasingly turn to private equity and overseas exchanges for capital. Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with ASX General Manager, Listings James Posnett about the rebound in new listings and what it means for Australia's capital markets. Plus, Mathan Somasundaram from Deep Data Analytics unpacks a flat session for the ASX as BHP shares fell amid strike action at its Port Hedland iron ore operations, while AMP and REA Group rallied on upbeat company updates.

item.title: New Zealand tsunami alert for South Island cancelled after magnitude-5.9 earthquake
item.description: The earthquake shaking was "long and loud" and sounded "like a train".

item.title: See the images: Australia's 'devastating' problem that's visible from space
item.description: The rain brought green to the Red Centre — but for some, it revealed a landscape under threat.

item.title: One city is falling behind in Australia's home ownership divide
item.description: Home ownership has held steady nationally, new data suggests. But one city has slipped to its lowest rate in nearly 70 years.

item.title: The dramatic shift in a longstanding Australian addiction
item.description: New data on Australians' drug use is out. There's encouraging news, but a rising trend has public health experts worried.

item.title: INTERVIEW: The insidious danger of 'pump and dump' financial scams
item.description: ASIC is warning Australians to be extremely cautious of investment tips received through social media and messaging apps, amid a spike in reports of so-called 'pump and dump' scams using fake celebrity endorsements and impersonations of financial institutions. ASIC says scammers use the identities and images of well-known finance industry figures, including respected market commentators and economists, to lure consumers into WhatsApp and other messaging groups where they are encouraged to buy investments, particularly shares. Once consumers join a messaging group, scammers provide stock tips designed to artificially inflate the price of a share before selling their own holdings. The share price then falls, leaving unsuspecting investors with significant losses. SBS Reporter Natalie Poyhonen has been talking to ASIC Commissioner Alan Kirkland

item.title: Argentina are one win from glory — and fans are turning to lucky rituals
item.description: A healthy dose of superstition is helping Argentina fans cope with the pressure ahead of the World Cup final.

item.title: 'You can almost taste the smoke': Heavy smoke covers parts of North America as Canada battles wildfires
item.description: Hundreds of wildfires burning in Canada have caused dangerous air quality levels throughout Canada and large parts of the United States. Air quality has plummeted to hazardous levels in parts of North America, as officials and health experts urge residents to avoid the outdoors.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 17 July 2026
item.description: A daily 5 minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: Government expected to rebuff key recommendations to tackle Islamophobia
item.description: It’s taken ten months for the government to respond to the report handed down by the special envoy to combat Islamophobia.

item.title: Government expected to rebuff key recommendations to tackle Islamophobia
item.description: It’s taken ten months for the government to respond to the report handed down by the special envoy to combat Islamophobia.

item.title: Why one man's dismissal has triggered rare wartime protests in Ukraine
item.description: Mykhailo Fedorov, a 35-year-old tech expert, is out as Ukraine's defence minister after a surprise government reshuffle.

item.title: Why Pauline Hanson's appearance on Tommy Robinson's podcast is so controversial
item.description: Pauline Hanson's podcast with a far-right UK activist has sparked backlash — and a defence from Barnaby Joyce.

item.title: Miners drag the ASX lower as SpaceX slips below IPO price
item.description: The Australian sharemarket ended the week on a softer note, with the ASX 200 falling half a per cent on Friday as heavy losses among mining stocks dragged the market lower, leaving the benchmark down 0.1 per cent for the week. Meanwhile, in the United States, SpaceX shares slipped below their US$135 IPO price, leaving investors who bought at the float and held the stock sitting on a paper loss. Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG Markets, joins the podcast to unpack the day's biggest market moves and what they mean for investors.

item.title: Unis lacked 'trauma-informed' care around Hamas-Israel war, royal commission hears
item.description: Education leaders have argued that they need to balance freedom of speech and academic protections.

item.title: Unis lacked 'trauma-informed' care around Hamas-Israel war, royal commission hears
item.description: Education leaders have argued that they need to balance freedom of speech and academic protections.

item.title: Signs of slowing Chinese economy and Middle East conflict jangle investor nerves
item.description: It's been a tense week for Australian investors, beset by uncertainty on all sides.

item.title: 'It would be a disaster': The threat unfolding inside this Pacific map
item.description: As Pacific tuna harvests are projected to decline, community-led solutions may prove key to saving local fishing grounds.

item.title: Meet the families bringing hidden histories out of the shadows
item.description: Almost 400,000 people in Australia claim Dutch ancestry. Yet many of their migration stories remain hidden. An exhibition and a new book aim to change that. This story was produced in collaboration with SBS Dutch.

item.title: 'Game changer': New blood test could enable Alzheimer's detection years before symptoms
item.description: A blood test may predict the start of Alzheimer's symptoms years in advance and doctors say it could become a game changer. The study shows a blood test can help identify healthy people at high risk for Alzheimer's disease and how far their disease has progressed.

item.title: An alert will sound across the country on July 27. Advocates worry it will place some women at risk
item.description: On July 27, a loud alarm will blare from every mobile phone in Australia. It’s part of testing for the new AusAlert system, which will inform people about emergencies in their area, including natural disasters, public safety threats and bio-security incidents. But there are concerns the alert could put victim-survivors of family and domestic violence with hidden phones at risk.

item.title: Trump blames Canada for wildfire smoke blanketing the US, demands compensation
item.description: The US president has complained that the country is being "invaded by filthy air" while the Canadian PM asks for assistance.

item.title: Muslim leader welcomes govt's Islamophobia envoy response despite 'one glaring omission'
item.description: The federal government will adopt 35 of the 54 recommendations handed down by the special envoy to combat Islamophobia.

item.title: The Australian who feels 'used as a political football' after Argentina's World Cup stunt
item.description: Australian Sally Poncet has lived in the remote island community for more than 40 years.

item.title: Albanese issues Opposition a One Nation 'test' at launch of Islamophobia report response
item.description: Angus Taylor has been challenged to follow the lead of former prime minister John Howard in how he handles Pauline Hanson.

item.title: Laos says lack of autopsies means cause of tourist deaths cannot be proven as methanol
item.description: Those indicted over the deaths are facing charges related to selling food harmful to health and operating an illegal business.

item.title: DFAT making urgent inquiries after Australian tackled and handcuffed by ICE at US airport
item.description: The Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade said it would provide consular assistance to any Australian citizen who requested it.

item.title: Australia's coal capital is preparing for what once seemed 'unimaginable'
item.description: A once-in-a-lifetime change is taking place on the sites of old coal-fired power stations.

item.title: Tuna Wars: Local communities vital to sustain fishing grounds
item.description: Some Pacific Island Nations are facing a decline in tuna harvest, by 20 per cent on average, by 2050 - with experts warning climate-related fish migration could pose a looming threat to food security and economies across the region. In Solomon Islands, more than 90 per cent of inshore coastal areas fall under customary marine tenure - casting community-led solutions as vital to the sustainability of local fishing grounds. And this feature was produced with support from the Pulitzer Centre.

item.title: What happens when coal mines close and renewable energy takes over?
item.description: The Hunter region of New South Wales has long been associated with coal mining and energy production. As the transition to renewable energy ramps up, key players are trying to make sure there's enough jobs for workers who will be displaced. And taking advantage of the sites that played a key role in coal-fired energy generation, for a renewable future.

item.title: United States launches new strikes on Iran after two US soldiers killed by Iranian attack
item.description: New waves of strikes from the US and Iran have claimed more lives, with both sides taking aim at the Strait of Hormuz again.

item.title: Could Alzheimer’s be predicted years before symptoms? A test offers new clues
item.description: A 'game changer' test for Alzheimer's could soon be on the way, doctors say.

item.title: Hanson tells UK conservative conference audience to 'not apologise for being white'
item.description: One Nation Party leader Pauline Hanson said the UK had massive migration problems and she was seeing similar issues in Australia.

item.title: After rapidly losing 10kg, I was given a diagnosis that changed my relationship with food
item.description: Doctors couldn't find an answer to Ben Hampton's debilitating symptoms — then a friend's suggestion changed his life.

item.title: Australia has an unlikely tourist attraction — and fans are spending thousands to reach it
item.description: Some fans are travelling from across the world and spending thousands for an experience lasting three hours.

item.title: Australia has an unlikely tourist attraction — and fans are spending thousands to reach it
item.description: Some fans are travelling from across the world and spending thousands for an experience lasting three hours.

item.title: US, Iran launch missile attacks; two US personnel killed | Morning News Bulletin 20 July 2026
item.description: US troops killed as the Iran conflict intensifies; an earthquake strikes Peru's Andes region; the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina kicks off.

item.title: 'Impossible': Nauru community detainees say Australian funds not enough to buy food with
item.description: They face a choice between paying for food, water or internet.

item.title: Spain wins FIFA World Cup | Midday News Bulletin 20 July 2026
item.description: Royal Australian Air Force launches largest international exercise; fresh strikes between the US and Iran; Spanish fans delighted by World Cup win.

item.title: The World Cup is over. Here's where your football fix comes from next
item.description: The World Cup has ended, but star-studded club competitions are within reach.

item.title: Ukraine calls for more air defence systems as Russia bombards Ukrainian cities
item.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy is urging allies to provide more air defence systems after another wave of Russian attacks on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities. The major barrage of Russian drones, ballistic missiles and guided bombs killed at least six people and wounded dozens.

item.title: In pictures: How the world watched the World Cup final
item.description: As the final match of the 2026 World Cup tournament played out, fans around the world came together and celebrate the beautiful game.

item.title: In pictures: How the world watched the World Cup final
item.description: As the final match of the 2026 World Cup tournament played out, fans around the world came together and celebrate the beautiful game.

item.title: SBS News in Easy English 20 July 2026
Major international military exercise kicks off in the Top End
item.description: The Royal Australian Air Force’s largest international exercise is underway in the Northern Territory and will run for the next three weeks. The Prime Minister has described this year as the most significant for Exercise Pitch Black, owing to increased the importance of international alliances.

item.title: Trump hails "patriots" killed by Iran | Evening News Bulletin 20 July 2026
item.description: Trump describes military personnel killed in Jordan and Iraq as "great patriots"; asylum seekers going hungry on Nauru; French striker Kylian Mbappé takes out the Golden Boot at the World Cup.

item.title: Asylum-seekers on Nauru facing food, water shortages
item.description: Thirteen years after Australia resumed offshore processing on Nauru, a new report has found people seeking asylum on the island are struggling to afford enough food. The findings are based on interviews with 78 of the approximately 91 people living under the regional processing arrangement.

item.title: Changing of the guard: The young stars taking over from Messi, Ronaldo, and Neymar
item.description: As a generation of legends bows out empty-handed, meet the young stars set to define football's next decade.

item.title: Strong super returns meet a new oil price shock
item.description: Australian superannuation funds have delivered another year of strong gains, with Chant West reporting the median growth option returned 9.5 per cent in the last financial year, lifting cumulative returns to 44 per cent over four years. Mano Mohankumar, Head of Super Investments at Chant West, explains what drove the better-than-expected performance and why diversification continues to pay off. Plus, Raymond Chan, Head of Asian Desk at Morgans, unpacks a flat session for the Australian sharemarket as technology stocks weakened on China's latest AI developments while energy shares rallied after Brent crude climbed above US$90 a barrel, and explains what he's telling clients amid the latest market volatility.

item.title: Israel's embassy takes rare swipe at Labor's platform on eve of conference
item.description: The four-page statement drew a shrug from a member of Labor's front bench.

item.title: A dark chapter of Australia's past is resurfacing in immigration politics
item.description: Australia ended the White Australia policy decades ago. A new immigration debate has revived concern about its ideas.

item.title: A dark chapter of Australia's past is resurfacing in immigration politics
item.description: Australia ended the White Australia policy decades ago. A new immigration debate has revived concern about its ideas.

item.title: Britain has a new Prime Minister | Morning News Bulletin 21 July 2026
item.description: Andy Burnham formally becomes UK prime minister; US President Trump vows to avenge the killing of US soldiers; celebrated English footballer Kevin Keegan dies at the age of 75.

item.title: Yemen's Houthis declare Saudi maritime blockade, threatening new front in US-Iran war
item.description: The Saudi-led coalition in Yemen said it will respond to the Houthis' threats to ships with "force".

item.title: Andy Burnham promises a 'circuit breaker' for Britain
item.description: The UK’s new Prime Minister, Andy Burnham has pledged to bring stability to the country’s politics, declaring his premiership will be a ‘circuit breaker for Britain’. Speaking in Downing Street, the former mayor of Manchester, who only re-entered parliament a month ago, promised a new economic model - but acknowledged his plans will take time.

item.title: 'Kings of the World': Spain's World Cup stars get a hero's welcome from fans and royals
item.description: Spain's World Cup-winning football team were greeted by the country's royals, followed by a bus parade through Madrid.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Is Andy Burnham the answer Britain didn't know it was looking for?
item.description: Andy Burnham took office on Monday as Britain’s new prime minister, promising a 10-year plan to restore political stability and revive the economy while acknowledging that six other UK leaders have tried and failed to do so in the past decade. Speaking after being formally appointed by King Charles III, Burnham pledged to ease the cost of living for ordinary people, decentralize political power, revitalise industry and end street sleeping by homeless people. He said he would deliver a “circuit breaker” for a system that has taken wrong turns since the 1980s, when public services and utilities were privatized and local government eroded. Tim Bale, professor of politics at Queen Mary University of London, told the Associated Press that Labour may have backed Burnham - but he faces some challenges to persuade the rest of the country to do the same.

item.title: INTERVIEW: ASIC tackles corporate misconduct, collects hundreds of millions of dollars in fines
item.description: The Australian Securities and Investments Commission says it has secured a record $830 million in civil penalties orders during the 2025-2026 Financial Year. It launched more than 250 investigations, which led to 25 criminal convictions and 11 individuals being sentenced to prison. Businesses subjected to major civil penalties include Union Standard International Group, HSBC Bank Australia, Westpac, Walker Stores and Mercer Super. SBS Reporter Stephanie Youssef has been speaking with ASIC Commissioner Alan Kirkland

item.title: Netanyahu may visit New York. Mamdani and Trump disagree on what awaits him
item.description: Israel has said the world court's arrest warrant for its prime minister was "bogus".

item.title: Netanyahu may visit New York. Mamdani and Trump disagree on what awaits him
item.description: Israel has said the world court's arrest warrant for its prime minister was "bogus".

item.title: 'Unpredictable': New warning for Australians travelling abroad
item.description: Escalating conflict could disrupt flights and travel plans, the government warns.

item.title: 'Unpredictable': New warning for Australians travelling abroad
item.description: Escalating conflict could disrupt flights and travel plans, the government warns.

item.title: The US applies a 50 per cent tariff on some Canadian products | Midday News Bulletin 21 July 2026
item.description: The United States applies a 50 per cent tariff on a range of Canadian products, Saudi Arabia has condemned a naval blockade declared by Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis. A Former FIFA referee says its too soon for a decision on expanding the World Cup ...

item.title: Who are India's 'Cockroach' Party? The youth movement leading Modi's biggest challenge in years
item.description: In one of the largest displays of public dissent against the Modi government, tens of thousands of protesters have descended on India's parliament to demand the education minister resign. Led by the rapidly growing youth-led "Cockroach" movement, demonstrators are calling for changes to what they say is a corrupt and broken education and employment system.

item.title: What is India's 'cockroach' movement? The anger behind its rapid rise
item.description: A student-led movement in India has grown into a broader campaign for accountability and restoration of trust in institutions.

item.title: US imposes tariffs on Canadian goods | SBS News in Easy English 21 July 2026
item.description: A news bulletin for English language learners

item.title: Hanson quizzed on Putin praise; says Rinehart has 'done it tough'
item.description: The One Nation leader was also pressed over her European holiday and comments about the White Australia policy.

item.title: Hanson quizzed on Putin praise; says Rinehart has 'done it tough'
item.description: The One Nation leader was also pressed over her European holiday and comments about the White Australia policy.

item.title: Hanson quizzed on Putin praise; says Rinehart has 'done it tough'
item.description: The One Nation leader was also pressed over her European holiday and comments about the White Australia policy.

item.title: US launches tenth consecutive day of attacks against Iran | Evening News Bulletin 21 July 2026
item.description: US forces have launch tenth consecutive day of attacks against Iran, The Islamic Council of Victoria has comdemns desecration of Muslim graves on Melbourne's outskirts The AFLW defends its choice of ground for Ireland matchup

item.title: Oil eases despite Red Sea threat as Kimi K3 shakes up AI
item.description: SBS Finance Editor Ricardo Gonçalves speaks with Mark Gardner from MPC Markets as the ASX finished flat yet again, despite the escalating conflict in the Middle-East and explores how China's newest AI tool is shaking up the industry.

item.title: Two years into the government's social housing push, a new report says it's well behind target
item.description: A new report from the Australian National Audit Office has cast doubt on the pace of the government's social housing builds.

item.title: Bigger, richer and messier: The World Cup that reshaped FIFA's showpiece
item.description: Cape Verde's rise, record crowds and billion-dollar revenues defined the World Cup, but so did controversy on and off the pitch.

item.title: 'It's really going to hurt': The oil route helping keep prices down is now under threat
item.description: If Yemen's Houthi rebels successfully blockade Saudi Arabian ships, global oil supplies could come under more pressure.

item.title: Trump says US will ‘take care’ of Red Sea blockade | Morning News Bulletin 22 July 2026
item.description: Donald Trump downplays the threat of Iran-backed Houthis blockading the Red Sea; Foreign Minister Penny Wong meets with her Lao counterpart to discuss the investigation into the deaths of two Australians; La Liga's president calls for Gianni Infantino to resign as the head of FIFA.

item.title: Gen Z is the first generation in history in which men are more religious than women
item.description: Religious affiliation has been in decline for decades. Now, a religious divide is appearing among gen Z men and women.

item.title: New funding hopes to shine light on energy bills for migrant communities
item.description: The New South Wales Government is investing two million dollars in community organisations to help people better understand their energy bills and reduce household costs. The funding will support programs working with culturally diverse communities, people with disability, regional communities and social housing tenants.

item.title: World Cup 2026 wrap: Highlights, controversies and the future
item.description: New stars emerged alongside established legends at the FIFA World Cup 2026™ . Then there were the moments that had supporters on the edge of their seats. FIFA's unexplained decision on US key player Folarin Balogun's red card, after US President Donald Trump urged FIFA President Gianni Infantino to review the incident. VAR controversies, criticism of hydration breaks, concerns over the treatment of Iran's national team, denying the entry of Somali referee Omar Artan to the US. What can the Socceroos takeaway from the tournament? Join your host Haylena Krishnamoorthy for this final episode on World Cup Daily | The 90+ Podcast, as she breaks down some of these concerns with former FIFA referee, Christina Unkel. Was the expanded 48-team format worth it? Did VAR actually improve the tournament?

item.title: Flights to return after 40-year hiatus as Trump promises to help Lebanon 'a lot'
item.description: Lebanese President Joseph Aoun says his goal is for "a complete Israeli withdrawal from Lebanese territory".

item.title: 'Business-friendly socialism': What are new UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham's policies?
item.description: From energy cuts to rent controls to Brexit, here's what the United Kingdom's new prime minister wants to do.

item.title: 'Business-friendly socialism': What are new UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham's policies?
item.description: From energy cuts to rent controls to Brexit, here's what the United Kingdom's new prime minister wants to do.

item.title: INTERVIEW: Who are the Houthis?
item.description: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis say they're imposing a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, a move that opens a new front against the United States in its war on Iran and widens the threat to global energy supplies and trade beyond the Gulf. Iran wanted the Houthis to close the Bab el-Mandeb gateway to the Red Sea if the U.S. continued to attack Iranian power infrastructure. The US says it will 'take care' of the Houthis. So who are the Houthis? Farea al-Muslimi is a research fellow at Chatham House's Middle East and North Africa program. He's told Reuters they dominate Yemen.

item.title: 'Strong concerns': Australia meets with China following ballistic missile test
item.description: Penny Wong reiterated the importance of transparency to her Chinese counterpart.

item.title: Australian to launch new national warning system | Midday News Bulletin 22 July 2026
item.description: Australians urged to prepare for a test run of the country's new national warning system, One Nation MP Barnaby Joyce apologises for yelling at his dog, Remco Evenepoel wins the stage 16 time trial at the Tour de France.

item.title: Europe simmers through heatwave conditions with fire and droughts sweeping parts of the continent
item.description: Large parts of Europe are continuing to swelter through heatwave conditions as wildfires burn out of control in multiple countries. The world's fastest-warming continent, Europe is becoming increasingly prone to heatwaves, droughts and destructive wildfires.

item.title: Former Socceroo becomes first Australian footballer to share probable CTE diagnosis
item.description: There are growing calls for restrictions on football heading after Paul Wade revealed he likely has a neurodegenerative disease.

item.title: New National alert system about to be launched | SBS News in Easy English 22 July 2026
item.description: A five minute bulletin for English language learners

item.title: The phone alert that could reveal a hidden lifeline
item.description: Millions of phones will sound an emergency alert. For some, that noise could have consequences.

item.title: The phone alert that could reveal a hidden lifeline
item.description: Millions of phones will sound an emergency alert. For some, that noise could have consequences.

item.title: Millions of Australian customer details potentially exposed after Origin Energy hacked
item.description: The company said it was aware of an incident in which customer data may have been accessed by an unauthorised party.

item.title: US completes 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran | Evening News Bulletin 22 July 2026
item.description: US completes 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran, Concerns the Federal Government will not meet social and affordable housing target, Jessica Fox claims two gold in two days at canoe slalom world champs.

item.title: 'Terrible advice': Advocates call out Hanson's domestic violence 'two-way street' comments
item.description: Advocates and experts said the comments were ignorant of the realities domestic and family violence victims face.

item.title: The age Australians think is 'too old' to still live with your parents
item.description: Australians have drawn a line on when adults should leave the family home. The rental market is making that harder.

item.title: The age Australians think is 'too old' to still live with your parents
item.description: Australians have drawn a line on when adults should leave the family home. The rental market is making that harder.

item.title: The age Australians think is 'too old' to still live with your parents
item.description: Australians have drawn a line on when adults should leave the family home. The rental market is making that harder.

item.title: 'Truth-teller': Rebel Wilson victorious in defamation trial brought by The Deb co-star
item.description: Actor Charlotte MacInnes originally claimed Wilson had defamed her in a series of social media posts that implied she was a liar and a sellout.

item.title: Oil hits a six week high as investors finally move the ASX
item.description: The Australian sharemarket rose 0.3 per cent after investors sat on their hands for two session. Stephanie Youssef speaks with Luke Laretive from Seneca Financial Solutions to go through the day's moves, including another tick higher in the oil price and he's take on AI.

item.title: Minister says IS-linked doctor should 'rot in jail' after his discovery in Iraq
item.description: It was assumed that paediatrician Tareq Kamleh had been killed in Syria in 2017.

item.title: Minister says IS-linked doctor should 'rot in jail' after his discovery in Iraq
item.description: It was assumed that paediatrician Tareq Kamleh had been killed in Syria in 2017.

item.title: Minister says IS-linked doctor should 'rot in jail' after his discovery in Iraq
item.description: It was assumed that paediatrician Tareq Kamleh had been killed in Syria in 2017.

item.title: Shady's back: New twist in Eminem's legal stoush with Australian swimwear brand
item.description: The Australian company won the first round against Eminem. But the rapper's trademark battle is not yet over.

item.title: 2026 Census: When it is, how to complete it and who must take part
item.description: Avoid the last-minute scramble with this quick Census guide.

item.title: 2026 Census: When it is, how to complete it and who must take part
item.description: Avoid the last-minute scramble with this quick Census guide.

item.title: Danni was diagnosed with bowel cancer at 39. It made her rethink what she ate growing up
item.description: Australians are some of the world's biggest consumers of ultra-processed foods. But Danni has cut almost all UPFs from her diet.

item.title: US, Saudi Arabia Announce Nuclear Deal | Morning News Bulletin 23 July 2026
item.description: Saudi Arabia and US announce deal to develop nuclear power; Two bodies recovered after PNG boating incident; Former Socceroos captain Paul Wade reveals traumatic brain injury.

item.title: 'Exact opposite symbolism': Hitler's birthplace transformed to deter extremists
item.description: The building, in an Austrian town near the German border, has been remodelled to "prevent any association with Adolf Hitler".

item.title: The superannuation 'blind spot' funding Australian gambling companies
item.description: Australians are losing more money per capita to gambling than any other country — about $32 billion a year.

item.title: Yemen's Houthis claim Red Sea tanker strikes, as Trump threatens Iran's power plants
item.description: The escalations intensify the risk of further pressure on global oil supply and prices.

item.title: A nuclear-sized risk? The US-Saudi deal changing the equation in the Middle East
item.description: A landmark agreement has revived longstanding concerns, and raised fresh questions about the Middle East's nuclear future.

item.title: A nuclear-sized risk? The US-Saudi deal changing the equation in the Middle East
item.description: A landmark agreement has revived longstanding concerns, and raised fresh questions about the Middle East's nuclear future.

item.title: Midday News Bulletin | Yemen's Houthis launch attacks on Saudi oil tankers
item.description: Prime Minister stresses cost of living at national Labor conference, Yemen's Houthis claim strikes on Saudi oil tankers in the red sea, Australia’s flag and baton bearers named for the 2026 Commonwealth Games...

item.title: Full list: Here's who will get $338 weekly payments under prac payment expansion
item.description: The prime minister said it is "surprising" such a scheme supporting allied health students doesn’t already exist.

item.title: Houthis attack two Saudi oil tankers | SBS News in Easy English 23 July 2026
item.description: A daily five-minute news wrap for English learners and people with disability.

item.title: 'Literally terrifying': Does this 'rogue' experiment offer a glimpse of our future?
item.description: An autonomous AI agent escaped its testing environment and hacked another technology platform during internal capability experiments.

item.title: Hanson says domestic violence comments taken 'out of context' as criticism mounts
item.description: The One Nation leader has pinned the blame on media outlets covering comments she made that domestic violence is a "two-way street".

item.title: Hanson says domestic violence comments taken 'out of context' as criticism mounts
item.description: The One Nation leader has pinned the blame on media outlets covering comments she made that domestic violence is a "two-way street".

item.title: Australia raises concerns over Chinese missile test at ASEAN | Evening News Bulletin 23 July 2026
item.description: Foreign Minister Penny Wong raises concerns over a Chinese missile test at ASEAN; Ebola death toll in eastern Congo surpasses 1,000; And in sport, Wallabies prepare for clash against Japan.

item.title: AusAlert: What to expect from Australia's new emergency warning system
item.description: Australia is preparing to introduce a new national emergency warning system, designed to deliver critical alerts faster and more reliably during disasters. Whether it's a bushfire, flood or cyclone, timely warnings can give people precious time to act. Ahead of its first nationwide test, we take a closer look at how it works, what Australians can expect and why some emergency agencies still have concerns about the rollout.

item.title: Houthi’s Red Sea blockade, India’s cockroach uprising & hundreds of baby Haarlands?
item.description: Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi forces have declared an immediate maritime blockade against Saudi Arabia, threatening to choke off the strategic sea route and further destabilise global energy markets. With US-Iran tensions already at a boiling point, will this latest escalation plunge the Middle East into a broader regional war? Plus, from satire to a massive student uprising, could India’s “Cockroach" movement test Modi’s power? And the post-World Cup baby craze sweeping Peru.

item.title: Zelenskyy has faced years of war. Now comes a test from within
item.description: A military shake-up has eased the unrest for now, but revealed the political strains building as the war against Russia drags on.

item.title: Origin Energy reports customer bank, credit card details caught up in data breach
item.description: After advising it had been caught up in a data breach on Wednesday, Origin Energy has revealed the full extent of stolen materials.
